labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1.1 Market Orientation vs. Product Orientation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) explicitly asks to distinguish market-oriented vs product-oriented approaches.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 4: Marketing Management - The 7 P's, strategies and forecasting > 4.5 The seven Ps of the marketing mix > 4.5.4 Promotion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.5.4 Promotion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) is about through-the-line promotion and how the campaign reflects it, fitting promotion within the marketing mix.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 4: Marketing Management - The 7 P's, strategies and forecasting > 4.2 Marketing planning > 4.2.3 Niche Market vs. Mass Market
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.2.3 Niche Market vs. Mass Market
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) asks whether AstraPod operates in a niche or mass market.

parts.content:
Calculate:

(i) Creditor days

(ii) Depreciation expense using the straight-line method
parts.markscheme:
(a)

(i) Creditor days = (Trade creditors ÷ Annual credit purchases) × 365 = (150 ÷ 1,200) × 365 ≈ 45.6 ≈ 46 days. <marks>1 mark</marks>

(ii) Straight-line depreciation = ($1,500m ÷ 15 years) = $100m/year. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the information provided, distinguish between a market-oriented and product-oriented approach.
parts.markscheme: (b) Product orientation = focus on internal design and innovation regardless of market needs. Market orientation = responsiveness to customer feedback and demand. AstraPod has historically used a product-oriented approach but may shift toward market orientation as it targets public contracts. <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Identify one feature of through the line promotion and explain how AstraPod's campaign reflects this.
parts.markscheme:
(c) Through the line combines above the line (national ads) and below the line (influencer-led content).

AstraPod's campaign used both mass media and targeted social engagement, thus qualifies as through the line. <marks>2 marks</marks>

parts.content: Outline whether AstraPod is currently operating in a niche market or mass market
parts.markscheme:
(d) AstraPod currently serves tutoring centres and private schools <marks>1 mark</marks> a clearly defined, specialized market = niche. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying niche, and further <marks>1 mark</marks> for development

parts.content: Explain one limitation of AstraPod Ltd.'s current depreciation method
parts.markscheme:
(e) Straight-line depreciation assumes uniform usage, which may not reflect equipment wear patterns. <marks>1 mark</marks>

A units-of-production method might better match asset value loss to actual production output. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for valid explanation and further <marks>1 mark</marks> for development.

questions.specification:
**EcoFreight Solutions**

EcoFreight Solutions is a **private limited company (Ltd)** specializing in zero-emission urban logistics and electric vehicle (EV) fleet management. The firm prioritizes environmental sustainability while aiming for a target return on capital employed (ROCE) of 8% to reinvest in next-generation battery technology.

**Table 1: Statement of Profit or Loss for EcoFreight Solutions for the year ending 31 December 2024** *(figures in $000)*

| Item | Amount ($000) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Sales revenue | 4,200 |
| Cost of sales | 2,400 |
| Operating expenses | 1,100 |
| Depreciation expense | 150 |
| Interest expense | 60 |
| Tax | 80 |

**Table 2: Additional Financial Information**

| Item | Amount ($000) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Capital employed | 5,500 |
| Current assets | 920 |
| Current liabilities | 800 |
| Initial investment for new project | 1,200 |
| Net annual cash inflow from project | 300 |

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.5.3 Liquidity Ratios
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) requires calculating the current ratio, a liquidity ratio.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.8.1 Investment Appraisal Techniques
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) asks for the payback period, which is an investment appraisal technique.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 3: Financial Management - Accounting, Ratio evaluation, Core Financial Documents > 3.7 Cash flow > 3.7.2 Working Capital
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.7.2 Working Capital
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) focuses on 90-day payment terms creating working capital pressure (slow receivables leading to cash shortfalls).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 5: Operations Management - Production Methods, Production planning and management > 5.8 Research and development (HL only) > 5.8.1 Importance of Research and Development (R&D)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.8.1 Importance of Research and Development (R&D)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) concerns eligibility for government R&D tax credits, linking to the importance/nature of R&D activity (e.g., developing next-generation battery technology).

parts.content: Calculate the gross profit for EcoFreight Solutions (in $000). Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct method: $\text{gross profit}=\text{sales revenue}-\text{cost of sales}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $4{,}200-2{,}400=1{,}800$ (in $000) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State one reason why EcoFreight Solutions’ activities could meet typical criteria for government R&D tax credits.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies qualifying R&D activity involving innovation/technical uncertainty (e.g., developing/improving battery technology, charging optimisation, fleet-management software/telematics) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links to creation/improvement of a product/process beyond routine operations (OWTTE) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the current ratio for EcoFreight Solutions. Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct method: $\text{current ratio}=\frac{\text{current assets}}{\text{current liabilities}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\frac{920}{800}=1.15$ so current ratio $=1.15:1$ (or $1.15$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the payback period for the automated sorting hub project (in years). Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct method: $\text{payback period}=\frac{\text{initial investment}}{\text{net annual cash inflow}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $\frac{1{,}200}{300}=4$ years <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain one cash-flow or liquidity challenge that EcoFreight Solutions may face as a result of relying on long-term corporate contracts with 90-day payment terms.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies a relevant challenge such as slower cash inflows/receivables build-up leading to working-capital pressure (OWTTE) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains impact, e.g. may need overdraft/short-term finance, risk of missing payments to suppliers/wages, higher finance costs, reduced liquidity <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
ClearWave Ltd. is a UK-based start-up that designs and installs water purification systems for rural communities and NGOs. Its long-term **business objectives** include expanding into three African markets by 2026, maintaining ethical sourcing practices, and achieving an annual growth rate of 20%.

To support its **growth and evolution**, the company secured a **government development grant**, a **bank loan**, and retained profits. However, some **stakeholders**—particularly NGO clients and community partners—have raised concerns about whether expansion could compromise product quality and service support.

ClearWave’s finance team has shared Q2 2024 **final accounts**, alongside the business’s **budgeted figures**, to assess how growth pressures are impacting profitability.

—


**Table 1: Budgeted vs Actual Figures – Q2 2024**

| Item                      | Budgeted (£) | Actual (£) |
|---------------------------|--------------|------------|
| Sales revenue             | 1,250,000    | 1,100,000  |
| Cost of goods sold        | 600,000      | 670,000    |
| Operating expenses        | 420,000      | 430,000    |
| Net profit                | 230,000      | —          |

---

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.9.3 Variances
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (b) require calculating sales revenue variance and total cost variance from budgeted vs actual figures, which is budget variance analysis.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 3: Financial Management - Accounting, Ratio evaluation, Core Financial Documents > 3.4 Final accounts > 3.4.2 Final Accounts
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.4.2 Final Accounts
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) uses final accounts logic (income statement components: sales, COGS, operating expenses) to compute actual net profit.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.2.2 External Sources of Finance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) asks for an additional source of finance to support growth, which focuses on external financing options.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 3: Financial Management - Accounting, Ratio evaluation, Core Financial Documents > 3.2 Sources of finance > 3.2.1 Internal Sources of Finance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.2.1 Internal Sources of Finance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The prompt references retained profits as an existing funding method, relating to internal sources of finance (context for finance choices).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 1: Introduction to Business Management - Core operational components and business functions > 1.4 Stakeholders > 1.4.1 Internal and External Stakeholders
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.4.1 Internal and External Stakeholders
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) asks for one internal and one external stakeholder group and why they are concerned about expansion.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 1: Introduction to Business Management - Core operational components and business functions > 1.3 Business objectives > 1.3.2 Common Business Objectives
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.3.2 Common Business Objectives
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The case states long-term business objectives (market expansion, ethical sourcing, growth rate) and part (c) links financial performance to achieving these objectives.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.5.3 Reasons for Businesses to Grow
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) analyses how current performance affects ability to meet growth targets, aligning with reasons/pressures for business growth and implications.

parts.content:
Calculate the total costs variance (cost of goods sold + operating expenses) and the sales revenue variance for ClearWave Ltd. in Q2 2024. State whether each variance is favourable or adverse.
Show all your working.

Calculate the actual net profit for Q2 2024.
Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Net profit $= 1{,}100{,}000 - 670{,}000 - 430{,}000 = 0$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct final answer (actual net profit is £0) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyse how ClearWave’s current financial performance may affect its ability to meet its growth objectives.
parts.markscheme:
- Limited/no retained profit reduces internal funding available for expansion <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May delay/limit ability to invest in capacity (e.g., staff, offices, service support), making 20% growth harder to achieve <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest one internal and one external stakeholder group that may be concerned about the company’s expansion and explain why.
parts.markscheme:
- Internal stakeholder identified (e.g., employees) with a relevant reason linked to expansion (e.g., workload, resourcing, job security) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- External stakeholder identified (e.g., NGO clients/community partners/investors) with a relevant reason linked to expansion (e.g., quality/service concerns; returns/risk) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme:
- Valid additional source of finance identified (e.g., crowdfunding/ethical investment platform; angel investor; leasing) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- One clear advantage explained in context (e.g., aligns with social mission; provides expertise; spreads cost/minimises upfront cash outlay) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Calculate the total cost variance and the sales revenue variance for ClearWave Ltd. in Q2 2024.  
Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
Variance calculations:

- **Sales variance** = 1,100,000 – 1,250,000 = **–£150,000 (adverse)**  
- **Total cost variance** = (670,000 + 430,000) – (600,000 + 420,000)  
  = 1,100,000 – 1,020,000 = **+£80,000 (adverse)**

Award [1] for correct working, [1] for both accurate answers.  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content:
Calculate the actual net profit for Q2 2024.  
Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
Net profit calculation:

- **Net profit** = 1,100,000 – 670,000 – 430,000 = **£0**  

Award [1] for correct method, [1] for accurate final value.  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content: Analyse how ClearWave’s current financial performance may affect its ability to meet its growth objectives.
parts.markscheme:
Impact on growth objectives:  
- No profit limits internal funding capacity for expansion  
- Poor performance may deter future investors or delay loan approvals  
- Resource constraints could affect ability to open new regional offices or recruit  
- Growth targets (e.g., 20% annual increase) are harder to meet without profitability  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content: Suggest one internal and one external stakeholder group that may be concerned about the company’s expansion and explain why.
parts.markscheme:
Stakeholder concerns:  
- **NGO clients**: May worry about reduced service quality or responsiveness as the firm expands too fast  
- **Employees**: Concerned about workload and job security during cost-cutting  
- **Investors**: May be concerned about lack of return and deviation from financial projections  
Award [1] for identifying a stakeholder, [1] for relevant reasoning.  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content: Suggest one additional source of finance ClearWave Ltd. could consider to support future growth, and explain one advantage of your choice.
parts.markscheme:
Source of finance suggestion:  
- **Option**: Crowdfunding or ethical investment platform  
- **Advantage**: Maintains alignment with ClearWave’s social mission and appeals to value-driven supporters  
Other valid options:  
- **Angel investors** – often offer mentorship alongside funding  
- **Leasing** – spreads out large capital costs with minimal upfront expense  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

questions.specification:
**NovaShades Ltd.**

NovaShades Ltd. produces customisable smart sunglasses that adjust tint based on sunlight. The company is in its third year of operations and is seeking to expand its presence into two new markets. A recent round of **market research** revealed a growing demand among athletes and frequent travellers, prompting changes to the **marketing plan**, including new promotional strategies and adjusted pricing.

To fund the launch, the business secured a **short-term bank loan** and reinvested a portion of last year’s retained profit. The leadership team promotes a **task-oriented management style**, focused on achieving efficiency, though recent internal feedback shows that some employees feel **undervalued** and disconnected from the company’s vision.

The finance team has provided partial data for Q2 2024 and asked the marketing interns to complete the missing values in the **statement of profit or loss** using the table below.

**Table 1: Financial data for NovaShades Ltd. (Q2 2024)**

| Item                               | Amount ($) |
|------------------------------------|------------|
| Units sold (units)                 | 2,000      |
| Selling price ($ per unit)         | 120.00     |
| Variable cost ($ per unit)         | 45.00      |
| Salaries (marketing + operations)  | 40,000     |
| Advertising and promotion          | 18,000     |
| Office rent and utilities          | 15,000     |
| Loan interest                      | 2,500      |
| Tax rate                           | 25%        |
| Dividends paid                     | 10,000     |

**Figure 1: NovaShades Ltd. statement of profit or loss for the quarter ended 30 June 2024**

|                           |      **\$** |
|---------------------------------------------|------------:|
| Sales revenue                               |    _______ |
| Cost of sales                               |    _______ |
| **Gross profit**                            |    _______ |
| **Expenses:**                               |             |
|   Salaries                        |   (40,000) |
|   Advertising and promotion       |   (18,000) |
|   Office rent and utilities       |   (15,000) |
| **Profit before interest and tax**          |    _______ |
| Interest                                    |    (2,500) |
| **Profit before tax**                       |    _______ |
| Tax (25%)                                   |    _______ |
| **Profit for the period**                   |    _______ |
| Dividends                                   |   (10,000) |
| **Retained profit**                         |    _______ |

parts.content: Suggest one possible reason why the leadership style at NovaShades Ltd. might be contributing to employee demotivation.
parts.markscheme:
- Task-oriented leadership may prioritise efficiency/targets over employee recognition and involvement. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how the findings from NovaShades Ltd.’s market research could be used to adjust its marketing planning.
parts.markscheme:
- Market research can identify target segments/customer needs and preferences to inform marketing decisions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme:
- Promotion (e.g., increased advertising and promotion). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This supports entry into new markets by building awareness and targeting athletes/travellers through appropriate channels/messages. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: **Using the data in Table 1**, complete Figure 1 (statement of profit or loss). **Show your working.**
parts.markscheme:
**Complete the statement of profit or loss:**

**Working:**
- Sales revenue $= 2{,}000 \times 120 = 240{,}000$
- Cost of sales $= 2{,}000 \times 45 = 90{,}000$
- Gross profit $= 240{,}000 - 90{,}000 = 150{,}000$
- Total expenses $= 40{,}000 + 18{,}000 + 15{,}000 = 73{,}000$
- Profit before interest and tax $= 150{,}000 - 73{,}000 = 77{,}000$
- Profit before tax $= 77{,}000 - 2{,}500 = 74{,}500$
- Tax $= 25\% \times 74{,}500 = 18{,}625$
- Profit for the period $= 74{,}500 - 18{,}625 = 55{,}875$
- Retained profit $= 55{,}875 - 10{,}000 = 45{,}875$

**Completed statement**

NovaShades Ltd. Statement of profit or loss for the quarter ended 30 June 2024

| **Description**                             |      **\$** |
|---------------------------------------------|------------:|
| Sales revenue                               |     240,000 |
| Cost of sales                               |    (90,000) |
| **Gross profit**                            |     150,000 |
| **Expenses:**                               |             |
|   Salaries                        |    (40,000) |
|   Advertising and promotion       |    (18,000) |
|   Office rent and utilities       |    (15,000) |
| **Profit before interest and tax**          |      77,000 |
| Interest                                    |     (2,500) |
| **Profit before tax**                       |      74,500 |
| Tax (25%)                                   |    (18,625) |
| **Profit for the period**                   |      55,875 |
| Dividends                                   |    (10,000) |
| **Retained profit**                         |      45,875 |

Award marks for correct calculations/working and correct completion/formatting of Figure 1.

parts.content: Suggest whether the business appears financially stable in the short term (cash-flow resilience) based on its retained profit and its use of short-term finance.
parts.markscheme:
- The business generated a positive retained profit for the quarter ($45,875), suggesting it has an internal buffer to support operations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, reliance on a short-term bank loan could increase short-term cash outflows (repayments/interest), creating cash-flow risk if sales fall or costs rise. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
**Green Haven Ltd. (GH)** 

Green Haven Ltd. (GH) is a small private limited company that produces indoor hydroponic garden kits. GH was started by two former biology teachers with the aim of promoting sustainable food production in urban homes.

Initially, GH was funded through personal savings and a bank loan. In its first year of operation, GH earned $240,000 in revenue and incurred $160,000 in total costs. GH now aims to expand and is considering applying for a government grant to help fund the purchase of new machinery.

Table 1 shows selected financial information for GH.

**Table 1: Selected financial data for GH**

| Item                   | Amount ($) |
|------------------------|-------------|
| Revenue                | 240,000     |
| Total costs            | 160,000     |
| Net profit             | ?           |
| Loan interest (annual) | 5,000       |
| Owner investment       | 40,000      |

questions.specification:
### **Question 53 SkyGen Ltd.**

SkyGen Ltd. is a software company that previously operated using a traditional hierarchical structure with centralized decision-making. After losing several talented developers, the company began restructuring project teams and moving toward a flatter matrix system.

SkyGen has also updated its performance appraisal process and begun introducing self-managed development plans. The HR director is piloting new methods for evaluating staff performance while linking role enrichment to innovation output.

Below is SkyGen Ltd.'s Statement of Financial Position as at 31 December 2023.

Statement of Financial Position: SkyGen Ltd. (as at 31 December 2023) (All figures in $m)

| Non-current assets                           |  |       |
|----------------------------------------------|--|-------|
| -------------------------------------------- |  |       |
| Property, plant and equipment                |  | 1,800 |
| Accumulated depreciation                     |  | (600) |
| **Non-current assets**                       |  | 1,200 |
| Current assets                               |  |       |
| Cash                                         |  | 300   |
| Debtors                                      |  | 450   |
| Stock                                        |  | 250   |
| **Current assets**                           |  | 1,000 |
| **Total assets**                             |  | 2,200 |
| Current liabilities                          |  |       |
| Trade creditors                              |  | 200   |
| Short-term loans                             |  | 200   |
| **Current liabilities**                      |  | 400   |
| Non-current liabilities                      |  |       |
| Long-term borrowings                         |  | 300   |
| **Total liabilities**                        |  | 700   |
| **Net assets**                               |  | 1,500 |
| Retained earnings                            |  | 1,500 |
| **Total equity**                             |  | 1,500 |

Additional information:

- The PP&E figure includes $900m of specialized hardware expected to last 6 years.

- In 2023, that equipment was used to produce 15,000 units out of an expected 90,000-unit lifetime.

- Annual sales = $3,600m, of which 50% are credit sales.

- SkyGen previously used top-down summative appraisals but now trials peer-led feedback and project-based self-evaluations.

Answer all the questions.

(a) Calculate:

(i) Straight-line depreciation on the equipment

(ii) Units-of-production depreciation for the year

[2]

(b) Calculate SkyGen Ltd.'s debtor days.

[2]

(c) Distinguish between summative and self-appraisal, using SkyGen Ltd. as context.

[2]

(d) Explain how job enrichment and delayering might support SkyGen Ltd.'s move to a matrix structure.

[2]

(e) Identify one limitation of bureaucracy in SkyGen's previous structure and why a matrix structure may be more effective in its context.

[2]

Total marks: [10]

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 3: Financial Management - Accounting, Ratio evaluation, Core Financial Documents > 3.4 Final accounts > 3.4.4 Depreciation Methods (HL Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.4.4 Depreciation Methods (HL Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks for straight-line and units-of-production depreciation calculations, directly testing depreciation methods.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 3: Financial Management - Accounting, Ratio evaluation, Core Financial Documents > 3.6 Efficiency ratio analysis (HL only) > 3.6.1 Efficiency Ratios
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.6.1 Efficiency Ratios
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks to calculate debtor days, which is an efficiency ratio (accounts receivable days).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 2: HR Management - Motivation, Structure, Hierarchy > 2.4 Motivation and demotivation > 2.4.4 Types of Appraisal (HL Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.4.4 Types of Appraisal (HL Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) distinguishes summative (top-down) appraisal vs self-appraisal/peer-led/self-evaluation, which is covered under types of appraisal.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 2: HR Management - Motivation, Structure, Hierarchy > 2.2 Organizational structure > 2.2.4 Changes in Organizational Structures (HL Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.2.4 Changes in Organizational Structures (HL Only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (d) and (e) discuss delayering, moving from hierarchical/bureaucratic to flatter matrix structure and why it may be more effective—organizational structure change.

parts.content:
Calculate:

(i) Straight-line depreciation on the equipment

(ii) Units-of-production depreciation for the year
parts.markscheme:
(i) Straight-line depreciation = $900m ÷ 6 = $150m/year. 1 mark

(ii) Units-of-production depreciation = ($900m ÷ 90,000) × 15,000 = $150m. 1 mark

parts.content: Calculate SkyGen Ltd.'s debtor days.
parts.markscheme: Debtor days = (Debtors ÷ Annual credit sales) × 365 = (450 ÷ 1,800) × 365 ≈ 91.25 ≈ 91 days. 2 marks

parts.content: Distinguish between summative and self-appraisal, using SkyGen Ltd. as context.
parts.markscheme:
Summative = top-down review focused on final results, often used for ranking or performance-linked decisions.

Self-appraisal = employee-led reflection based on personal goals and feedback, supporting development.

SkyGen is shifting from summative to self-appraisal in its project teams.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for valid distinction and <marks>1 mark</marks> for explicit link to SkyGen Ltd.

parts.content: Explain how job enrichment and delayering might support SkyGen Ltd.'s move to a matrix structure.
parts.markscheme:
Job enrichment increases role complexity and autonomy — fits cross-functional matrix teams.

Delayering removes middle management, speeding decisions and improving accountability across overlapping roles.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying valid reason and <marks>1 mark</marks> for developing reason.

parts.content: Identify one limitation of bureaucracy in SkyGen's previous structure and why a matrix structure may be more effective in its context.
parts.markscheme:
Bureaucracy limits innovation through rigid chains of command and slow decision-making. A matrix structure allows SkyGen's employees to collaborate across departments and align faster with project goals.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying valid limitation and further <marks>1 mark</marks> for development of limitation.

questions.specification:
#### **Question 54 EcoBlade Ltd.**

EcoBlade Ltd. produces biodegradable razors aimed at environmentally conscious consumers. The company is deciding whether to invest in a second production site closer to its largest market. Management is considering two investment projects and recently reviewed its operational performance.

Table 1 shows income and expenses for the year ended 20XX. Table 2 presents data for two expansion projects under consideration.

Table 1: Income and Expenses for EcoBlade Ltd. – Year Ended 20XX (All figures in $m)

| Revenue | 14.0 |
|----------------------|-------|
| Cost of goods sold | 9.0 |
| Operating expenses | 3.0 |
| Net profit before tax | ___ |

| Current assets | 4.0 |
| Current liabilities | 2.0 |

Table 2: Investment Appraisal Data – Project A and Project B (All figures in $m)

| Project | A | B |
|----------------------|-------|-------|
| Initial investment | 5.0 | 5.0 |
| Net cash inflows: Y1 | 2.0 | 1.0 |
| Net cash inflows: Y2 | 2.0 | 2.0 |
| Net cash inflows: Y3 | 1.5 | 2.5 |
| ARR (%) (over 3 years) | ___ | ___ |
| NPV (@10% discount) | 0.7 | 0.5 |

Additional context:

- EcoBlade currently uses quality control to catch faulty razors at the packaging stage, but rejects remain high.

- The proposed new site would reduce delivery times by 50% and is near suppliers of plant-based plastic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 3: Financial Management - Accounting, Ratio evaluation, Core Financial Documents > 3.8 Investment appraisal > 3.8.1 Investment Appraisal Techniques
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.8.1 Investment Appraisal Techniques
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) require calculating ARR and evaluating NPV vs payback—core investment appraisal techniques.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 3: Financial Management - Accounting, Ratio evaluation, Core Financial Documents > 3.8 Investment appraisal > 3.8.2 Net Present Value (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.8.2 Net Present Value (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) specifically asks for an advantage of NPV, and the table provides NPVs at a discount rate.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 3: Financial Management - Accounting, Ratio evaluation, Core Financial Documents > 3.5 Profitability and liquidity ratio analysis > 3.5.3 Liquidity Ratios
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.5.3 Liquidity Ratios
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a)(ii) asks to calculate the current ratio using current assets and current liabilities (a liquidity ratio).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 3: Financial Management - Accounting, Ratio evaluation, Core Financial Documents > 3.4 Final accounts > 3.4.2 Final Accounts
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.4.2 Final Accounts
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a)(i) calculates net profit before tax from revenue, COGS, and operating expenses—income statement/final accounts content.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 5: Operations Management - Production Methods, Production planning and management > 5.3 Lean production and quality management (HL only) > 5.3.4 Quality Control vs. Quality Assurance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.3.4 Quality Control vs. Quality Assurance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) explicitly distinguishes quality control from quality assurance with an example.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Unit 5: Operations Management - Production Methods, Production planning and management > 5.4 Location > 5.4.1 Reasons for a Specific Location of Production
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.4.1 Reasons for a Specific Location of Production
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) asks for an operational reason for locating near suppliers—classic location-of-production factor.

parts.content: Calculate the net profit before tax.
parts.markscheme: - Net profit before tax $= \text{Revenue} - \text{COGS} - \text{Operating expenses} = 14.0 - 9.0 - 3.0 = 2.0$ ($m$). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the current ratio.
parts.markscheme: - Current ratio $= \text{Current assets} \div \text{Current liabilities} = 4.0 \div 2.0 = 2$ (i.e. $2:1$). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the ARR for both Project A and Project B. Assume the net cash inflows represent profit (ignore depreciation and any salvage value).
parts.markscheme:
- Uses $\text{ARR} = \left(\frac{\text{average annual profit}}{\text{initial investment}}\right)\times 100$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Project A: total inflows $= 2.0+2.0+1.5=5.5$; total profit $=5.5-5.0=0.5$; average annual profit $=0.5/3$; ARR $=\left(\frac{0.5/3}{5.0}\right)\times 100=3.33\%$ (approx). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Project B: total inflows $= 1.0+2.0+2.5=5.5$; total profit $=5.5-5.0=0.5$; average annual profit $=0.5/3$; ARR $=\left(\frac{0.5/3}{5.0}\right)\times 100=3.33\%$ (approx). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain one advantage of using NPV instead of payback period alone for investment decision-making.
parts.markscheme:
- NPV includes the time value of money (discounts future cash flows), which payback period does not. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Therefore it considers both the timing and size of returns (value created), not just how quickly the initial investment is recovered. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Distinguish between quality control and quality assurance using EcoBlade Ltd. as an example.
parts.markscheme:
- Quality control: inspection/testing to detect defects in finished output (e.g., EcoBlade checking razors at the packaging stage). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Quality assurance: planned process/procedures to prevent defects occurring during production (proactive, process-focused rather than product-focused). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline one operational reason why EcoBlade Ltd. is considering locating its second factory near suppliers.
parts.markscheme:
- Locating near suppliers can reduce transport distance/time and associated costs, improving reliability of supply/lead times. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This can also support sustainability aims (e.g., lower transport emissions) and improve responsiveness to production needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
**UrbanNest Ltd.**

UrbanNest Ltd. is a UK-based furniture company that designs and sells compact, modular pieces for urban apartments. Originally founded as a partnership, it restructured into a private limited company (Ltd.) to raise capital for growth. Its business objectives include increasing market share by 10% annually and maintaining a ROCE of 15%. Following strong domestic sales, UrbanNest is planning to launch a new e-commerce platform to support international expansion into Southeast Asia.

While revenue growth remains steady, rising marketing spend and inventory costs have placed pressure on liquidity. The finance team has shared final account data for 2024, revealing tight cash reserves and slower inventory turnover due to new customisable product lines. The company is now exploring funding options to support its expansion plan.


**Table 1: Selected Financial Data – UrbanNest Ltd. (2024)**

| Item                         | Amount (£)   |
|------------------------------|--------------|
| Revenue                      | 2,800,000    |
| Cost of goods sold           | 1,600,000    |
| Operating expenses           | 900,000      |
| Net profit                   | 300,000      |
| Capital employed             | 2,000,000    |
| Average stock                | 320,000      |

questions.specification:
**CraftCore Ltd.**

CraftCore Ltd. is a company that produces handmade wooden furniture. The company has grown rapidly in recent years and now employs over 100 staff. To manage this growth, CraftCore adopted a tall organizational structure, with multiple layers of management to improve oversight and communication.

The production manager uses a democratic leadership style and encourages team input in decision-making. Recently, management introduced a non-financial motivation scheme that includes job enrichment and employee involvement in quality control. These changes were made in response to a decline in staff engagement.

The finance department has provided the following data for the last quarter.

| Item                   | Amount ($) |
|------------------------|------------|
| Revenue                | 420,000    |
| Cost of goods sold     | 250,000    |
| Expenses               | 100,000    |
| Net profit             | ?          |

| Current liabilities    | 50,000     |

parts.content: Calculate the **net profit** for the quarter.  **Show all your working.**
parts.markscheme:
- Correct method shown: Net profit $=$ Revenue $-$ (Cost of goods sold $+$ Expenses) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $420{,}000-(250{,}000+100{,}000)=70{,}000$ so net profit $=\$70{,}000$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the **net profit margin**.  **Show all your working.**
parts.markscheme:
Net profit margin = (Net profit / Revenue) × 100  
= (70,000 / 420,000) × 100 = **16.67%**

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for correct formula and working, and <marks>1 mark</marks> for accurate final answer with percentage.  

<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the **current ratio**.  **Show all your working.**

- $75{,}000/50{,}000=1.5$ so current ratio $=1.5:1$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The company is considering investing \$90,000 in a flagship showroom. The showroom is expected to generate annual net cash inflows of \$35,000 for three years.  

**Calculate** the **payback period** for this investment.  **Show all your working.**
parts.markscheme:
Initial investment = \$90,000  
Annual net cash inflow = \$35,000  
Payback = 90,000 ÷ 35,000 = **2.57 years**

Award <marks>1 mark</marks>  for correct formula and method, <marks>1 mark</marks>  for correct final answer.  

<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

questions.specification:
**Elevate Health Tech (EHT)**

<dialog>
	
**Resource 1 – Background of the organization** 

Elevate Health Tech (EHT) is a social enterprise based in Peru. Founded in 2021 by two biomedical engineers, it develops **low-cost, portable diagnostic devices** (such as digital stethoscopes and glucose meters) for use in rural and underserved communities across Latin America. EHT reinvests all profits into R&D and local employment programs.

EHT has grown quickly, scaling from 5 to 38 employees in two years. It operates as a **private limited company**, with both founders holding equal ownership and decision-making authority.

</dialog>

<dialog>

**Resource 2 – Statement from the HR Director**

> "Our team is motivated by our mission, but we’re facing growing pains. We’ve recently hired 12 new staff, many with limited technical background. We have no formal **training programme or organizational structure**.  Employees report confusion about job roles, and productivity varies significantly across teams. The co-founders are very hands-on, but this is becoming unsustainable."
	
</dialog>

<dialog>
	
**Resource 3 – Loan proposal from the Development Impact Bank (DIB)**

> DIB is prepared to offer EHT a **$750,000 low-interest loan** to expand production and distribution across four Andean countries. The loan would cover machinery upgrades and recruitment but requires EHT to submit **quarterly financial statements** and maintain a **current ratio of at least 1.5** throughout the term.

> A grace period of 18 months will apply before repayments begin.

</dialog>

<dialog>
	
**Resource 4 – Excerpt from EHT’s financial summary (unaudited, 2024)**

| Item                                | Amount (USD)     |
|-------------------------------------|------------------|
| Current assets                      | $230,000         |
| Current liabilities                 | $180,000         |
| Non-current liabilities             | $50,000          |
| Retained profit                     | $40,000          |
| Total equity                        | $100,000         |

Note: Fixed assets are fully depreciated and no longer productive.

</dialog>

<dialog>
	
**Resource 5 – Email from the Head of R&D**

> "To remain competitive, we need to launch **two new diagnostic products** within the next 18 months. The current design lab is at capacity. We either need to **lease a second lab** or invest in automation. The team is overworked and we’ve lost two engineers to burnout. I’m also concerned about our lack of appraisal or performance review systems."

</dialog>

<dialog>
	
**Resource 6 – Mission statement (from company website)**

We believe affordable diagnostics are a human right. We exist to empower healthcare workers and communities with reliable tools, local jobs, and dignity-driven innovation.
	
</dialog>

parts.content: Explain one human resource challenge and one financial challenge that EHT may face if it accepts the DIB loan and scales up
parts.markscheme:
**Human Resource Challenge:**
- Onboarding many new staff without a **training system** (Resource 2).
- **Burnout and unclear roles** may worsen with expansion (Resource 5).
- Absence of **performance appraisal** limits employee development.

**Financial Challenge:**
- EHT’s current ratio is **1.28**, below the 1.5 required by DIB (Resource 4).
- The company may lack **working capital** to maintain liquidity while investing.
- Pressure to meet **quarterly reporting and loan covenants** could distract leadership.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a human resource challenge.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining the HR challenge.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for applying the HR challenge to EHT’s context.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a financial challenge.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining the financial challenge.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for applying the financial challenge to EHT’s situation.

parts.content: Using all the resources provided and your knowledge of business management tools and theories, recommend a possible plan of action for EHT over the next five years.
parts.markscheme:
**Note to examiners concerning synoptic assessment**

This is a synoptic assessment task and asks the candidate to:
- Integrate and apply content from **Topic 1 (Business structure and ownership), Topic 2 (Human Resource Management), and Topic 3 (Finance and accounts)**.
- Demonstrate **holistic business thinking** through a strategic five-year recommendation that reflects **ethical, operational, and financial feasibility**.
- Justify the chosen plan using **relevant tools and theories**, not just content recall.
- Engage with the **stimulus materials critically**, referencing specific figures, statements, or constraints.
- Evaluate realistic **trade-offs**, such as between speed of growth and liquidity, or social impact versus operational control.
- Present a clearly **sequenced plan** with interdependent actions (not isolated ideas).

**One possible plan of action:**  

**Accept the DIB loan + introduce formal HR systems + invest in lean expansion**

- **Accept the DIB loan**, but roll out expansion in **staged phases**. The initial 18-month grace period can be used to restore productivity and address capacity (Resource 3).  
- Improve the **current ratio** (currently 1.28) through careful **cash flow management**, delayed recruitment, and by **leasing** the second R&D lab rather than purchasing fixed assets (Resources 4 and 5).  
- Establish a **functional organizational structure** with clear delegation, especially as the co-founders currently manage all operations (Resource 2). Appointing a **COO** could alleviate leadership bottlenecks and enable founders to focus on strategy (Resource 1).  
- Implement HR systems: **training, appraisals, defined roles, and internal progression paths**. This would reduce burnout and improve retention (Resources 2 and 5).  
- Maintain alignment with the **social mission** by ensuring diagnostic tools remain affordable and community-centred (Resource 6), and by building **local talent pipelines** rather than relying solely on external recruitment.  
- Monitor **liquidity targets (1.5 current ratio)** closely through budgeting, forecasting, and inventory control. 

**Tools and Theories (must be integrated, not name-dropped):**

- **Organizational structures (functional, flat, matrix)** – Helps clarify roles, especially with rapid hiring and founder bottlenecks.
- **Motivation theory (Herzberg)** – Job dissatisfaction linked to absence of recognition, feedback, and role clarity (Resource 2).
- **Current ratio** – Quantitative reference (230,000 / 180,000 = 1.28). Strategic aim is 1.5 to meet DIB requirements (Resource 3).
- **Budgets and cash flow forecasting** – Needed to plan staged hiring and capital investments; supports lender reporting.
- **Make-or-buy analysis** – Leasing vs purchasing lab space; leasing reduces upfront strain on liquidity.
- **Internal vs external recruitment** – Choosing to upskill internal staff supports mission and loyalty but may slow scale-up.
- **Triple bottom line (TBL)** – Financial performance, community employment, and innovation for social good (Resource 6).
- **Investment appraisal (ARR or NPV)** – Optional but rewardable if used to compare automation vs lab expansion.

**Evaluation expectations:**

- Strong responses should explore **financial risk** (e.g. failing the current ratio covenant and losing access to DIB funds).
- Acknowledge that **scaling HR systems and onboarding** at speed is difficult without cultural dilution or mission drift.
- Consider whether **delegating to a COO** risks loss of founder control versus long-term scalability and efficiency.
- Discuss alternative options, such as seeking equity finance or impact investors instead of debt—especially given EHT’s social enterprise status and low gearing.
- Evaluate **short-term vs long-term implications** (e.g. delayed hiring may protect liquidity but risk burnout; rapid growth may boost visibility but harm retention).
- Final judgment should return to the mission: does the plan **support EHT’s ethical and operational commitments** while enabling sustainable growth?

<note> Responses that simply list actions or reword the stimulus without justification or synthesis should be capped at mid-band. High-band answers will integrate numerical analysis, mission alignment, and theory in support of a strategically sequenced plan. </note>

questions.specification:
UrbanSpice Café is a private limited company that operates a single café in the city centre. It is owned by two siblings who each invested $25,000 to start the business. Their aim is to provide a cosy environment with high-quality, locally sourced food and drinks.
The café has become increasingly popular, and the owners are now planning to open a second location. As part of this growth plan, they are reviewing financial data and considering how expansion may affect different stakeholders.

Table 1 shows UrbanSpice Café’s financial data for the most recent year (2024).
**Table 1: Financial data for UrbanSpice Café for 2024**
| Item                   | Amount ($) |
|------------------------|------------|
| Revenue                | 300,000    |
| Cost of goods sold     | 120,000    |
| Expenses               | 100,000    |
| Net profit             | ?          |
| Current assets         | 90,000     |
| Current liabilities    | 45,000     |

questions.specification:
**WKM**

WKM is a fast-growing agritech company that produces modular irrigation systems for small-scale farms. The company is exploring international expansion through partnerships with cooperatives in developing countries. 

As part of this move, the board is reviewing WKM’s current vision and mission statements to ensure they reflect its long-term goals and social impact values. Some directors have raised concerns about how operations in lower-income regions could affect the brand’s reputation and stakeholder expectations.

WKM is also considering changing its accounting method for depreciation—from straight-line to units of production—to better reflect asset usage across regions. This change could impact financial statements, employee bonuses, and decision-making around asset replacement. 

Management is evaluating whether the shift aligns with its strategic goals and provides more accurate cost control as the company scales globally.

parts.content: Apply your knowledge of depreciation methods to suggest why the units of production method might be more appropriate for WKM than the straight-line method.
parts.markscheme:

- More appropriate where production/usage varies across regions or over time (e.g. fluctuating demand/seasonal production), so wear and tear is not constant each year. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
NovaShell Ltd. is a manufacturer of biodegradable food packaging. The company has built a reputation for ethical production and environmental responsibility, supported by a **task-oriented corporate culture** that emphasises discipline, results, and continuous improvement.

Recently, tensions have risen between senior management and production staff following the introduction of stricter output targets and a reduction in paid breaks. These changes were implemented without union consultation, leading to a formal dispute and a breakdown in **industrial relations**. Several departments have seen a rise in absenteeism and declining morale.

At the same time, the finance team is reviewing the company’s **final accounts** and preparing a **cash flow forecast**. The operations team has proposed investing in a new press-moulding machine to reduce waste and lower unit costs. The company would need to seek external **finance** for this investment.

---

**Table 1: Financial Data – NovaShell Ltd (2024)**

| Item                                  | Amount (£) |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Revenue                               | 7,000,000  |
| Cost of goods sold                    | 4,000,000  |
| Operating expenses                    | 2,400,000  |
| Net profit                            | 600,000    |
| Capital employed                      | 4,800,000  |
| Average stock                         | 900,000    |
| Opening balance (cash)               | 50,000     |
| Forecasted inflows (Q3 2024)          | 420,000    |
| Forecasted outflows (Q3 2024)         | 460,000    |
| Cost of proposed investment           | 500,000    |

---

parts.content: Explain how NovaShell’s task-oriented corporate culture may have contributed to the breakdown in employee relations.
parts.markscheme:
Corporate culture and employee relations:  
- A task-oriented culture may prioritise results over employee wellbeing  
- Implementation of performance targets without consultation undermines trust  
- Employees may feel excluded from decision-making, leading to resentment  
- A rigid culture may be poorly suited to resolving industrial disputes collaboratively  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content:
Calculate the stock turnover ratio.  
Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
Ratio calculations:

- **Stock turnover** = 4,000,000 / 900,000 = **4.44 times**

Award [1] for correct formula and one accurate answer, [1] for both.  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content:
Calculate the net cash flow and closing balance for Q3 2024.  
Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
Cash flow calculations:

- **Net cash flow** = Inflows – Outflows = 420,000 – 460,000 = **–40,000**  
- **Closing balance** = Opening balance + Net cash flow = 50,000 – 40,000 = **£10,000**

Award [1] for correct method, [1] for accurate closing balance.  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content: Analyse how NovaShell Ltd.’s current efficiency and liquidity might affect its ability to secure external finance for the proposed investment.
parts.markscheme:
Financial performance and external finance:  
- ROCE of 12.5% is moderate, showing adequate returns  
- Stock turnover is reasonable, suggesting stable working capital management  
- Low closing cash balance indicates tight liquidity  
- Cash flow deficit and industrial unrest may concern lenders  
- May need to justify how investment will improve future returns and stability  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content: Suggest one way NovaShell’s leadership could rebuild trust with employees while maintaining operational efficiency.
parts.markscheme:
Leadership actions to restore trust:  
- Initiate transparent dialogue with employee representatives  
- Involve staff in reviewing productivity expectations  
- Balance efficiency with staff wellbeing (e.g. flexible break policies)  
- Offer professional development tied to operational goals  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

questions.specification:
**AstraPod Ltd.**

AstraPod Ltd. is a growing company that designs and sells interactive educational tablets aimed at a niche market: schools and tutoring centres. The firm recently launched a marketing campaign that combined online influencer-led explainers with national education magazine ads.

After steady early growth, the company now plans to scale into broader B2B contracts with public schools. It is considering adjusting its strategy from design-led innovation to more feedback-driven development.

Below is AstraPod Ltd.’s Statement of Financial Position as of 31 December 2024.

**Figure 1. Statement of Financial Position: AstraPod Ltd. (as at 31 December 2024)**  
*(All figures in $m)*

|                      | $m     |
|----------------------------------|--------|
| Property, plant and equipment    | 1,200  |
| Accumulated depreciation         | (200)  |
| **Net non-current assets**       | 1,000  |
| Cash                             | 250    |
| Debtors                          | 300    |
| Stock                            | 150    |
| **Total current assets**         | 700    |
| **Total assets**                 | 1,700  |
| Trade creditors                  | 150    |
| Bank overdraft                   | 50     |
| Short-term loan                  | 200    |
| **Total current liabilities**    | 400    |
| Long-term borrowings             | 300    |
| **Total liabilities**            | 700    |
| **Net assets**                   | 1,000  |
| Retained earnings                | 1,000  |
| **Total equity**                 | 1,000  |


Additional information:
- Annual credit purchases = $1,200m
- The factory and production machinery were purchased for $1,500m and are expected to last 15 years.
- AstraPod’s marketing campaign generated widespread social media engagement and national coverage.
- Product development has historically been led by the engineering team with limited market feedback.

- A units-of-production method might better match asset value loss to actual production output <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
**Zenith Electronics – Balancing Growth, HR, and Finance** **Resource 1 – News Article** Zenith Electronics faces growing financial strain amid rapid global expansion.
Zenith Electronics, a publicly traded multinational, specializes in manufacturing smart home technology. Over the past five years, the company has expanded operations across Europe and Asia, growing its workforce from 5,000 to 15,000 employees.
Despite its success, Zenith faces serious financial and HR challenges: - Declining profit margins due to rising costs of production and supply chain disruptions. - High employee turnover as workers feel undervalued and disengaged. - Managers struggle with organizational communication and workplace motivation, impacting productivity.
To address these issues, Zenith’s CEO, Lisa Wong, is considering two strategies:
**(1)** Restructuring the organization to improve efficiency and motivation.
**(2)** Securing external funding to support ongoing expansion and cash flow stability.
Investors remain divided on the best course of action. **Resource 2 – Internal Employee Survey Results**
*Recent HR data from Zenith Electronics employees*:
| **Issue** | **Percentage of Employees Concerned** | |---------------------------------|---------------------------------------| | Lack of motivation at work | 45% | | Weak communication from managers | 38% | | High workload and job stress | 50% | | Poor work-life balance | 42% |
Employees feel disconnected from leadership and lack incentives to stay engaged. **Resource 3 – Financial Report Summary**
*Zenith Electronics – Key Financial Data (Last Fiscal Year)* | **Financial Indicator** | **Value** | |-----------------------------|---------------| | Revenue | $800 million | | Net Profit | $50 million | | Gross Profit Margin | 38% | | Current Ratio | 0.8 | | Gearing Ratio | 55% |
**Key Concerns**: (1) Low liquidity (current ratio < 1) may threaten short-term financial stability.
(2) High gearing (55%) suggests heavy reliance on external finance.

parts.content: Using an appropriate business management theory, describe a human resources issue affecting Zenith Electronics.
parts.markscheme:
Zenith Electronics faces several HR challenges, including:
- **Motivation Issues** – Employees lack engagement, leading to high turnover.
- **Communication Barriers** – Weak managerial communication affects employee satisfaction.
- **Work-Life Balance Problems** – High workloads cause stress and burnout.

Reference to relevant HR theories should be rewarded.

Mark as <marks>1+1 marks</marks>.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for using an appropriate business management theory (e.g., Herzberg’s Two-Factor Theory, Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs, or Taylor’s Scientific Management) and <marks>1 mark</marks> for describing a HR issue affecting Zenith.

parts.content: Explain two financial challenges Zenith Electronics faces in maintaining both profitability and liquidity.
parts.markscheme:
Zenith faces several financial challenges:

**Low Liquidity (Current Ratio: 0.8)**
- The company may struggle to meet short-term liabilities, increasing financial risk.
- Application: With a current ratio below 1, Zenith risks cash flow problems.

**High Gearing (55%)**
- Heavy reliance on external finance increases interest costs and financial vulnerability.
- Application: A gearing ratio above 50% makes the company risky for investors.

**Declining Profit Margins**
- Rising costs of production and supply chain disruptions reduce profitability.
- Application: The company must optimize costs or increase prices to maintain profitability.

Mark as <marks>3+3 marks</marks>.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identification of a challenge, <marks>1 mark</marks> for explanation, and <marks>1 mark</marks> for application to Zenith Electronics.

parts.content: Using all the resources provided and your knowledge of business management, recommend a possible plan of action to ensure both financial stability and employee motivation at Zenith Electronics.
parts.markscheme:
Zenith must balance financial health and employee satisfaction to sustain growth.

**Strategic Plan:**
- HR & Motivation Strategies
	- Introduce performance-based incentives to increase motivation.
	- Implement work-life balance policies to reduce burnout.

- Financial Stability Measures
	- Improve cash flow management by negotiating better supplier payment terms.
	- Explore equity financing instead of debt to reduce gearing ratio.

- Operational & Organizational Adjustments
	- Conduct organizational restructuring to improve communication and efficiency.
	- Use investment appraisal techniques to ensure expansion is financially viable.

Criterion A: Use of resource materials
To what extent does the student use the resource materials provided to effectively support the recommended plan of action?

| Marks | Level Descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| **0** | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below. |
| **1** | Limited reference to the resource materials, or resources are used ineffectively. |
| **2** | Some reference to the resource materials, but in a superficial way. |
| **3** | Most of the resource materials are used to support the plan of action. |
| **4** | All resource materials are effectively used to support the plan of action. |

Criterion B: Business management tools and theories
To what extent does the student’s plan of action effectively apply appropriate business management tools and theories?

| Marks | Level Descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| **0** | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below. |
| **1** | Limited application of business management tools and theories. |
| **2** | Superficial application of business management tools and theories. |
| **3** | Satisfactory application of business management tools and theories. |
| **4** | Effective application of business management tools and theories. |

Criterion C: Evaluation
To what extent does the student effectively evaluate the expected impact of their plan of action on the relevant areas of the business?

| Marks | Level Descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| **0** | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below. |
| **1-2** | Largely descriptive, with limited analysis or evaluation. |
| **3-4** | Some evaluation of the expected impact, but not sustained. |
| **5-6** | Effective evaluation, considering trade-offs and business impact. |

Criterion D: Sequencing of ideas and plan of action
To what extent are the student’s ideas and plan of action sequenced in a clear and coherent manner?

| Marks | Level Descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| **0** | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below. |
| **1** | Limited sequencing of ideas and plan of action. |
| **2** | Ideas and a plan of action exist, but not always in a clear manner. |
| **3** | Effective sequencing of ideas and plan of action in a clear and coherent manner. |

**Green Haven Ltd. (GH)**

Green Haven Ltd. (GH) is a small private limited company that produces indoor hydroponic garden kits. GH was started by two former biology teachers with the aim of promoting sustainable food production in urban homes.

Initially, GH was funded through personal savings and a bank loan. In its first year of operation, GH earned \$240,000 in revenue and incurred $160,000 in total costs. GH now aims to expand and is considering applying for a government grant to help fund the purchase of new machinery.

Table 1 shows selected financial information for GH.

**Table 1: Selected financial data for GH**

| Item                      | Amount ($)      |

| Revenue                  | 240,000         |
| Total costs              | 160,000         |
| Net profit               | ?               |
| Loan interest (annual)   | 5,000           |
| Owner investment         | 40,000          |

parts.content: Define the term **business**.
parts.markscheme:
- A business is an organization that produces goods and/or provides services to satisfy customer needs.
- Most businesses aim to make a profit (unless they are non-profit or social enterprises).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a basic definition and <marks>1 mark</marks> for further detail or an example.

parts.content: Identify **two characteristics** of a private limited company (Ltd).
parts.markscheme:
- Has limited liability.
- Shares are privately held and not traded on a stock exchange.
- Usually has “Ltd” in its name.
- Owned by shareholders, often family or close associates.
- Cannot sell shares to the general public.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each relevant feature up to a maximum of <marks>2 marks</marks>.

parts.content:
Calculate GH’s **net profit** for its first year of operation.  
**Show all your working.**
parts.markscheme:
Calculate GH’s **net profit**.
Net profit = Revenue – Total costs
= $240,000 – $160,000
= $80,000

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for correct working and <marks>1 mark</marks>  for final answer with $ sign.

<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content: State one **short-term** and one **long-term** source of finance GH is using or considering.

Accept other valid benefits such as employee motivation or alignment with ethical investors.

questions.specification:
**GreenValley Organic (GVO)**

_GreenValley Organic_ (_GVO_) is a producer cooperative formed by twenty independent organic dairy farmers in a rural region. _GVO_ processes raw milk into premium cheeses and yogurts, which are sold to high-end boutique cafes and specialty grocery stores. Unlike traditional corporations, _GVO_ is owned and democratically controlled by the farmers themselves.

**Table 1: Selected financial data for _GVO_ for 2022 and 2023 (figures in $000s)**

|  | 2022 | 2023 |
| :--- | ---: | ---: |
| Sales revenue | 600 | 800 |
| Cost of goods sold | 300 | 440 |
| Gross profit | 300 | 360 |
| Net profit before tax | 80 | 96 |
| Total current assets | 120 | 180 |
| Total current liabilities | 60 | 75 |
| Inventory | 40 | 70 |
| Cash | 15 | 10 |

In early 2022, _GVO_ invested in a new automated packaging line to improve efficiency. While the members (the farmers) were pleased with the increased production capacity, the cooperative faced several challenges. Some members felt that the focus on automation was drifting away from their original mission of supporting traditional farming techniques.

By the end of 2023, _GVO_ considered expanding into international markets. To fund this expansion, some board members suggested converting the cooperative into a private limited company (_Ltd._) to attract external investors. However, many founding members fear this would lead to a loss of control over the cooperative’s democratic values and profit-sharing model.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) explicitly compares a cooperative to farmers operating as individual sole traders.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (0) requires calculation of gross profit margin (a profitability ratio).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.5.3 Liquidity Ratios
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) requires calculation of the current ratio (a liquidity ratio).

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) includes the motivation to convert to Ltd. to attract external investors, linking to external sources of finance.

parts.content: Calculate the gross profit margin for _GVO_ for 2022 **and** 2023.
parts.markscheme:
_Calculations:_

**2022:** 
$\frac{300}{600} \times 100 = 50\%$

**2023:**
$\frac{360}{800} \times 100 = 45\%$

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correct answer. Showing working is not required.

parts.content: Identify **two** characteristics of a cooperative.
parts.markscheme:
- Democratic control / one member, one vote <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Profits (surplus) are distributed to members based on their level of participation or usage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limited liability for its members <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Owned and run by its members (employees, consumers, or producers) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Common social or economic goal shared by members <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each characteristic identified up to a maximum of 2 marks.</note>

parts.content: Calculate the current ratio for _GVO_ for 2022 **and** 2023.
parts.markscheme:
**2022:** 
$\frac{120}{60} = 2.0$

**2023:**
$\frac{180}{75} = 2.4$

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correct answer. Showing working is not required. Accept ratios expressed as 2:1 or 2.4:1.

parts.content: Explain **two** advantages for the dairy farmers of operating as a cooperative rather than as individual sole traders.
parts.markscheme:
Marks are awarded holistically up to a maximum of 4.

Responses may include (OWTTE):
- **Bulk purchasing / economies of scale**: farmers can reduce average costs by buying inputs together or sharing services/equipment, improving profitability.
- **Shared marketing/processing/distribution power**: the cooperative can process, package and negotiate with buyers more effectively than an individual farmer, potentially improving prices received and market access.
- **Risk sharing**: losses/volatility are spread across members, improving individual financial security.

Markband guidance:
- **1–2 marks**: Identifies one or two relevant advantages with limited or generic explanation.
- **3–4 marks**: Explains two distinct advantages with clear, developed explanation linked to the context of GVO and its dairy farmer members.

parts.content: Discuss the proposal for _GVO_ to change its legal status from a cooperative to a private limited company (_Ltd._).
parts.markscheme:
- **Analysis of current position**:
  - GVO is growing (Revenue up from $600k to $800k).
  - Liquidity is improving (Current ratio increased from 2.0 to 2.4).
  - However, Gross Profit Margin fell from 50% to 45%, suggesting rising costs or efficiency issues despite the new packaging line.

- **Arguments for converting to a Private Limited Company (Ltd.)**:
  - **Access to Capital**: It is easier to attract external investors by selling shares. This is crucial for the proposed international expansion, which requires significant funding.
  - **Professional Management**: External investors may demand more professional management structures, potentially improving the falling profit margins.
  - **Stability**: A corporate structure might be more attractive to banks for securing long-term loans compared to a cooperative.

- **Arguments against converting (Staying a Cooperative)**:
  - **Loss of Control**: Farmers lose their democratic "one member, one vote" status. Wealthy external investors could dictate company policy, potentially ignoring the farmers' interests.
  - **Profit Distribution**: In a cooperative, surplus goes back to the producers. In an Ltd., dividends are paid to shareholders, which might reduce the income of the individual farmers.
  - **Mission Drift**: The "organic" and "traditional" values mentioned in the text might be sacrificed for profit maximization to satisfy external shareholders.

- **Conclusion/Judgment**:
  - If the expansion is essential for survival, the Ltd. structure offers the best financial path. However, if the farmers value their independence and the community-based mission of GVO, they should seek alternative funding (e.g., government grants or member loans) rather than converting and losing their identity.

<note>6 marks: Balanced analysis of both Ltd. and Cooperative structures.</note>
<note>7-8 marks: Good discussion with use of financial data and consideration of stakeholder conflict.</note>
<note>9-10 marks: Thorough discussion with a clear, justified judgment on whether the conversion is appropriate.</note>

questions.specification:
**AeroLite Ltd.**

AeroLite Ltd. is a mid-sized European manufacturer of lightweight drone components. In 2024, the company launched a new line aimed at hobbyists and small commercial operators. The marketing team developed a detailed promotional campaign based on prior survey results and projections. However, actual orders have been lower than anticipated, prompting concerns about the reliability of AeroLite’s planning processes.

The finance department has provided final accounts and operational data from the latest quarter. The senior management team is now reviewing sales forecasts, operational performance, and marketing effectiveness to plan for the next fiscal year.

---

**Table 1: Selected Financial Data – AeroLite Ltd. (Q2 2024)**

| Item                          | Amount (€)   |
|-------------------------------|--------------|
| Forecasted sales revenue      | 3,000,000    |
| Actual sales revenue          | 2,600,000    |
| Cost of goods sold (forecast) | 1,500,000    |
| Cost of goods sold (actual)   | 1,700,000    |
| Operating expenses            | 600,000      |
| Net profit (actual)           | —            |
| Capital employed              | 4,800,000    |
| Current assets                | 1,100,000    |
| Current liabilities           | 750,000      |

---

parts.content:
Calculate the sales revenue variance and the cost of goods sold (COGS) variance for AeroLite Ltd.  
Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
Variance calculations:

- **Sales revenue variance** = Actual – Forecasted = 2,600,000 – 3,000,000 = **–€400,000 (adverse)**  
- **COGS variance** = Actual – Forecasted = 1,700,000 – 1,500,000 = **+€200,000 (adverse)**

Award [1] for correct method, [1] for both accurate values.  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content:
Calculate the actual net profit for Q2 2024.  
Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
Net profit and net profit margin calculations:

- **Net profit** = 2,600,000 – 1,700,000 – 600,000 = **€300,000**  

Award [1] for correct method, [1] for accurate answer.  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content:
Calculate the current ratio for AeroLite Ltd.  
Show all your working.
parts.markscheme:
Financial ratio calculations:

- **Current ratio** = 1,100,000 / 750,000 = **1.47:1**  

Award [1] for correct formula, [1] for correct calculation.

parts.content: Comment on what these calculations reveal about AeroLite Ltd.’s marketing planning and operational efficiency.
parts.markscheme:
Commentary on financial and marketing performance:  
- Adverse sales variance suggests the marketing plan overestimated demand  
- Higher-than-expected COGS further reduced profit margins  
- Current ratio above 1.4 indicates acceptable liquidity but not overly strong  
- ROCE at 6.25% is low, indicating poor return on invested capital despite efforts to boost sales  
- Marketing planning, budgeting, and cost control need tighter integration  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

parts.content: Suggest two ways AeroLite Ltd. could use market research or revised sales forecasting to improve future financial performance.
parts.markscheme:
Suggestions for improving forecasting and research:  
- Use primary research methods (e.g., customer interviews, product testing) to refine market demand estimates  
- Incorporate more conservative or scenario-based sales forecasts to prevent overproduction and cost overruns  
- Analyse past campaign effectiveness and customer feedback to adjust marketing strategies dynamically  
- Adopt rolling forecasts that adjust regularly based on real-time sales data  
<marks>Full marks (2/2)</marks>

questions.specification:

The leadership team uses a **democratic management style**, regularly consulting middle managers for input on marketing campaigns and operational changes. A recent **market research report** revealed rising demand for quiet, minimalist air purifiers, especially among remote workers. In response, AeroPure adjusted its **product design, pricing, and promotion strategy**, targeting health-conscious professionals.

parts.content: Using Figure 1, comment on one implication this statement of financial position may have for AeroPure Ltd.’s working capital and cash flow strategy.

parts.content: Using Figure 1, calculate AeroPure Ltd.’s current ratio and acid test ratio. Show your working.

questions.specification:
**AeroTech Drones – Scaling Production While Maintaining Financial Stability** **Resource 1 – News Article** AeroTech Drones Faces Challenges Scaling Its Production Line
AeroTech Drones, a technology start-up specializing in high-performance drones for commercial use, has seen rapid demand growth in the last two years. The company was founded in 2018 as a private limited company, but it is now considering an IPO (initial public offering) to raise additional capital for expansion.
Despite strong revenue growth, AeroTech is facing operational bottlenecks and financial pressures.
**Key Challenges**:
**(1)** High production costs due to inefficiencies in manufacturing and inconsistent supplier lead times.
**(2)** Limited cash flow despite strong revenues, causing delays in R&D investments for next-generation drones.
**(3)** Difficulty in deciding whether to expand the current factory or relocate to a larger facility to accommodate increasing orders.
The CEO, Jake Mitchell, is considering three key strategies:
**(1)** Raising capital through an IPO or alternative external funding sources.
**(2)** Optimizing production efficiency through lean production techniques to reduce costs.
**(3)** Expanding into a new location with greater production capacity while balancing cost and operational feasibility. **Resource 2 – Financial Report Summary** *AeroTech Drones – Financial Data (Last Fiscal Year)*
| **Financial Indicator** | **Value** | |-----------------------------|---------------| | Revenue | $200 million | | Gross Profit Margin | 40% | | Net Profit Margin | 8% | | Current Ratio | 0.9 | | Gearing Ratio | 62% |
Key Insights:
**(1)** The low current ratio (0.9) suggests short-term liquidity risks, meaning the company may struggle to meet immediate obligations.
**(2)** The high gearing ratio (62%) indicates heavy reliance on debt financing, increasing financial risk if interest rates rise.
**(3)** Profit margins remain solid, but production inefficiencies are increasing costs, reducing overall profitability. **Resource 3 – Break-even Analysis for Factory Expansion** | **Option** | **Fixed Costs ($M)** | **Variable Cost per Unit ($)** | **Selling Price per Unit ($)** | **Break-even Output (Units)** | |--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------------| | Expand Current Facility | 20M | 200 | 400 | 100,000 | | Relocate to New Facility | 35M | 180 | 400 | 116,667 |
**Key Takeaways**:
**(1)** Expanding the current factory has lower fixed costs, but it may not support long-term growth.
**(2)** A new facility reduces variable costs, but has higher fixed costs and a higher break-even point.
